passages.explanation: A crowd of thousands gathers, so dense they're trampling each other. Jesus speaks first to his disciples: beware of the yeast of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy. There's nothing concealed that won't be disclosed. What you've said in the dark will be heard in the daylight. Don't be afraid of those who kill the body and after that can do no more. Fear the one who, after killing the body, has authority to throw you into hell. Aren't five sparrows sold for two pennies? Yet not one of them is forgotten by God. The very hairs of your head are numbered. Don't be afraid — you're worth more than many sparrows. Whoever publicly acknowledges me, the Son of Man will acknowledge before God's angels. Whoever disowns me will be disowned. Anyone who speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but anyone who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven. When you're brought before synagogues, rulers, and authorities, don't worry about how to defend yourselves — the Holy Spirit will teach you what to say. Jesus balances two fears: fear of man (don't) and fear of God (do). Then he removes the anxiety from both by pointing to God's care and the Spirit's help.

Someone in the crowd says: teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me. Jesus replies: who appointed me a judge or an arbiter between you? Then he warns them: watch out — be on guard against all kinds of greed. Life doesn't consist in an abundance of possessions. He tells the parable of the rich fool. A rich man's land produces a good crop. He thinks: I have no place to store my crops. I'll tear down my barns and build bigger ones, and I'll store all my grain and goods. Then I'll say to myself: you have plenty stored up for years to come — take it easy, eat, drink, and be merry. But God says: you fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you. Then who will get what you've prepared? This is how it will be with anyone who stores up things for themselves but is not rich toward God. The man's mistake isn't wealth — it's that his entire plan is a monologue with himself. God doesn't appear in his thinking until God appears to end it.

Jesus says to his disciples: don't worry about your life — what you'll eat — or about your body — what you'll wear. Life is more than food and the body more than clothes. Consider the ravens: they don't sow or reap, they have no storeroom or barn, yet God feeds them. How much more valuable are you than birds? Who of you by worrying can add a single hour to your life? Since you can't do this little thing, why worry about the rest? Consider the lilies — they don't labour or spin, yet Solomon in all his splendour wasn't dressed like one of these. If that's how God clothes the grass, which is here today and tomorrow thrown into the fire, how much more will he clothe you — you of little faith! Don't set your heart on what you'll eat or drink — don't worry about it. The pagan world runs after these things, and your Father knows you need them. Seek his kingdom, and these things will be given to you as well. Don't be afraid, little flock — your Father has been pleased to give you the kingdom. Sell your possessions and give to the poor. Provide purses that won't wear out — a treasure in heaven that won't be exhausted. Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also. The logic isn't naive optimism — it's that if God handles the big things (life, kingdom), he can handle the small things (food, clothing). Worry is a misallocation of trust.

Be dressed ready for service and keep your lamps burning, like servants waiting for their master to return from a wedding banquet. It will be good for those servants whose master finds them watching when he comes — he'll dress himself to serve, have them recline at the table, and come and wait on them. If he comes in the middle of the night or toward daybreak and finds them ready, it will be good for them. If the owner of the house had known at what hour the thief was coming, he wouldn't have let his house be broken into. You must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour when you don't expect him. Peter asks: Lord, are you telling this parable to us or to everyone? Jesus answers with another parable: who then is the faithful and wise manager whom the master puts in charge? If the master finds him doing so when he returns, he'll put him in charge of all his possessions. But if that servant says 'my master is taking a long time' and begins to beat the other servants and to eat and drink and get drunk, the master will come unexpectedly and cut him to pieces. The servant who knows his master's will and doesn't get ready or doesn't do what the master wants will be beaten with many blows. The one who doesn't know and does things deserving punishment will be beaten with few blows. From everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded. Greater knowledge means greater accountability.

Jesus says: I've come to bring fire on the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled! I have a baptism to undergo, and what constraint I'm under until it is completed! Do you think I came to bring peace on earth? No, I tell you, but division. From now on, five in one family will be divided — three against two and two against three. Father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother. Jesus turns to the crowd: when you see a cloud rising in the west, you immediately say it's going to rain, and it does. When the south wind blows, you say it'll be hot, and it is. Hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of earth and sky — how is it you don't know how to interpret this present time? Why don't you judge for yourselves what is right? As you're going with your adversary to the magistrate, try hard to be reconciled on the way — or you may be dragged before the judge, turned over to the officer, and thrown into prison. I tell you, you won't get out until you've paid the last penny. Jesus shatters the peaceful-teacher image. His coming forces decisions that divide families. And the urgency is real — settle your accounts before it's too late.
